original_abstract:
Cultivating prosociality and character remains a primary objective for contemporary educational institutions. This study, conducted as part of an experimental investigation into the effects of ethics education, explores how pupils perceive their own prosocial behavior compared to evaluations provided by their peers and teachers. Utilizing a revised version of Roche’s Prosocial Behavior Scale, the researchers collected data from a sample of 254 fifth- and sixth-grade pupils (aged 10–12) at selected primary schools in Slovakia.

The findings reveal significant discrepancies in how prosociality is perceived, particularly along gender lines. The data suggest that teachers hold more polarized views than the students themselves, evaluating girls as more prosocial than the girls’ own self-assessments, while viewing boys more critically than the boys perceive themselves. Generally, boys tended to rate their own prosociality higher than their peers did, whereas this discrepancy was less pronounced among girls. Age also emerged as a relevant factor for educators, who viewed younger (fifth-grade) pupils significantly more positively than sixth graders. Regarding family structure, while living in a single-mother household showed no distinct impact on these perceptions, children living only with their fathers exhibited markedly lower self-evaluations of prosociality compared to how they were perceived by others. These results highlight the influence of subjective biases and social contexts in the assessment of moral development within the school environment.
humanized_abstract:
Social behaviour and character formation are still considered to be fundamental goals of present-day schools and educational systems. This paper is a continuation of an experimental research investigating the effects of ethics education on socialisation of pupils. The main goal of this paper is to present findings of how pupils evaluate their prosocial behaviour in relation to the evaluations given by their peers and teachers. The empirical part of the paper is based on the modified version of Roche’s Prosocial Behavior Scale. The research sample consisted of 254 fifth- and sixth-grade pupils (aged 10–12) from a few randomly selected primary schools in Slovakia.

Sex differences in teachers’ evaluations of students’ prosocial behaviour are reported based on a study (carried out in Finland) that investigated teacher and pupil evaluations of pupils’ prosocial behaviour. Several biases were observed in teachers’ and pupils’ evaluations, and which were different for different evaluation criteria, sexes and age groups. Teachers had more extreme evaluations of pupils’ prosocial behaviour than pupils had of themselves, with girls being evaluated as more prosocial by teachers than pupils evaluated themselves as being, and with boys being evaluated as less prosocial by teachers than pupils evaluated themselves as being. The boys seemed to overestimate their prosocial behaviour, whereas the girls seemed to underestimate it. Teachers evaluated fifth-graders more positively than sixth-graders. A single-mother family did not differ from other family structures, but pupils living only with their fathers were evaluated by teachers and pupils as being less prosocial towards others than pupils living with both parents.
